content: 156. Statement of Aryan Adhikari:

On Bhadra 23, the day called by the Gen-Z generation, I was in the Patan Industrial Area and was not an eyewitness. Hearing that the state had killed children by firing during the anti-corruption movement, I felt I should join and left my room around 10:30 AM on Bhadra 24. I went toward the Nakkhu Jail area hearing that Rabi Lamichhane was being released. Around 3:00 PM, when Rabi Lamichhane drove away in a vehicle, the crowd followed. The crowd and vehicle went together to Nakkhu Dobato Ring Road, after which the vehicle sped ahead, and I followed the crowd to Jawalakhel Chowk. There, I helped rescue 2-3 protesters who had collapsed from tear gas. In the evening, while moving toward Jawalakhel Chowk after having snacks, I saw protesters coming from Lagankhel who had seized an APF 'one-ton' van. They were planning to attack the Jawalakhel police. About 20-25 of us youth were trying to block the van, saying we shouldn't attack the police. Around 7:00–7:30 PM, the lights suddenly went out, and in the darkness, the police opened fire. I was shot in the right thigh and my right wrist. Sanjay Ghising, whom I know slightly, was there and dragged me to a house gate. Seeing the police following us, we tried to move again, but I fell. More shots were fired, and Sanjay was hit in the hand. We tried to reach Alka Hospital but saw another person fall from a police bullet. We hid in an alleyway in a local house for a while. Later, Sanjay's brother arrived on a motorcycle, and we reached Patan Hospital. I was hit by live rounds. I was hospitalized for eight days. The government bore all my medical expenses.
157. Statement of Smriti Rai:

I was a full participant in the Gen-Z protest on Bhadra 23, 2082. Around 10:30 AM, the rally was moving peacefully toward New Baneshwar, and I was at the back of the crowd. We reached the south gate of the Parliament building. We requested a designated space for a peaceful sit-in, but the administration ignored us and instead used baton charges and tear gas. The use of water cannons further angered the crowd. I saw about 10-15 protesters climb onto a small building inside the south gate. I saw one man on the roof bleeding and reached there to help lower him with the assistance of 2-3 others. He was bleeding from his head and legs, and the blood got onto my jacket. As I shouted toward the police, 'Who gave you the order to fire on the people's children?', a bullet came from in front and hit me in two places below my right knee. I screamed for help, and my friends took me to Civil Hospital in an ambulance. I was hit by rubber bullets. I was hospitalized for three days. My health is now normal, and the government covered all expenses.
158. Statement of Ayush V.K.:

I spontaneously participated in the Gen-Z protest on Bhadra 23, 2082. I was injured that day and did not participate on the 24th. When we reached the police barricades near the Everest Hotel, the police blocked us. I was at the front near the barricade. Protesters at the back began pushing forward, causing the front line to crash into the barricades. After some pushing, the police and APF started using water cannons and tear gas. Protesters began picking up tear gas canisters and throwing them back at the police. Eventually, the crowd broke the barricade. As the police retreated while firing, the crowd became more agitated and pelted stones. Protesters were being hit by rubber bullets. Around 12:35 PM, I was hit by a rubber bullet on the left side below my waist. Friends took me to Civil Hospital. I was told I had been hit by nine rubber bullets. Due to police intervention near Civil Hospital, my family transferred me to T.U. Teaching Hospital, Maharajgunj. Three rubber bullets are still inside my body. I am under follow-up. I believe the vandalism on the 24th was due to infiltration by other groups. I also want to report that various groups are using the names of the injured to collect funds nationally and internationally for their own benefit, and I request this be controlled.
159. Statement of Krishna Sunar:

I spontaneously participated in the Gen-Z protest on Bhadra 23, 2082. I was injured and thus not present on the 24th. I reached the south gate of the Parliament building around 12:00 PM via Ramshahpath. By the time I arrived, the protest had already turned violent. Around 1:00 or 2:00 PM, a bullet from an unknown direction hit me, and I lost consciousness. I woke up at Civil Hospital around 3:30 PM. I was shot near the shoulder joint above my left chest. I had surgery that day to remove one metal bullet, and another surgery on the 25th to remove four pellets (shrapnel). Doctors say two more pellets remain and will be removed later. Currently, my left hand does not function well. Civil Hospital referred me to T.U. Teaching Hospital because of nerve damage. The government has paid for my treatment and provided NPR 20,000, though I have had to buy some medicines personally, costing about NPR 8,000 so far. I believe the vandalism on the 24th was the work of infiltrators. I request action against the then-Prime Minister, Home Minister, CDO, and field commanders who ordered the firing, as well as those involved in the arson on the 24th.
160. Statement of Manoj Bahadur Bohara:

The protest was initially peaceful but became aggressive. As far as I remember, security forces gave no prior warning or appeal for dialogue. I was shot in the limbs and head. the distance between security forces and protesters was about 10–12 meters. I don't know how I was treated as I was unconscious, but I saw no police surveillance at the hospital. I lost my mobile phone and cannot provide the video I had recorded. The state used excessive force irresponsibly. I believe there was widespread infiltration in the movement; Gen-Z would not burn down Singha Durbar or courts. Those responsible must be found and punished. Leaders should be selfless and responsible, and those who suppressed the movement and the corrupt should be held accountable. The then-government must take full responsibility.
161. Statement of Sanjit Yadav:

I spontaneously joined the protest in front of the Parliament building in Baneshwar. It was peaceful at first, but the atmosphere turned violent after the police fired tear gas. There was a significant security presence, but they gave no warning before using force. I was injured in the leg by a bullet and water pressure from about 10 meters away. My treatment was delayed. A major incident occurred due to state suppression. Such events could be avoided through corruption control and public awareness. There was infiltration on the 24th; the guilty should be identified and punished.
162. Statement of Krishna Thapa:

On Bhadra 23, I was in front of the Parliament Building in Baneshwar, and on the 24th, I was in the Dhobighat and Kalimati areas. I spontaneously joined the movement called by Balen Shah to protest against corruption and the misuse of taxes, and to advocate for good governance. The protest was unrestrained. Security personnel fired tear gas. No appeal for dialogue was made, and no information regarding restricted zones was provided. There was a heavy security presence. While attempting to assist and protect the police, I was pushed by the crowd into a police beat, where I suffered an electric shock and injured my hand. the distance between the police and security personnel was 12 meters. I am unaware of the specifics of my treatment, which took place under police surveillance. I have some photos and videos. The state committed suppression; I demand action against corrupt individuals and the provision of education, health, and employment for the victims.
163. Statement of Yubaraj Thapa:

I spontaneously joined the protest at the front lines in the Baneshwar area around 12:00 PM to protest against the looting of the people. The crowd was aggressive. Security personnel provided information about the restricted zone but did not appeal for dialogue. I was struck with batons on my buttocks and chest. I received treatment. I do not have any photos or videos. The government suppressed us. I seek compensation, policy reform, and judicial action through the Commission. All those involved in the suppression should be hanged. There was infiltration from political parties.
164. Statement of Surendra Jung Thapa:

I spontaneously participated in the protest in front of the Baneshwar Parliament Building at 12:30 PM to oppose corruption. The protest was peaceful. The police and APF presence was moderate. I was not aware of any information regarding restricted zone barriers before force was used, as those zones had already been breached. I was shot in the leg, elbow, and thigh. My treatment was obtained independently. I have photos and videos. I heard orders to 'shoot, shoot.' I do not know the ranks of those involved. The government suppressed us. I demand compensation, policy reform, judicial action, and I seek an apology. There was infiltration by political parties.
165. Statement of Dipen Gurung:

I spontaneously joined the protest in Dillibazar to demand good governance and action against corrupt individuals. I did not see the Army, and security personnel gave no warning. I was shot in the chest. I do not have photos or videos. Treatment was obtained independently. I heard orders to 'shoot, shoot,' but I do not know the ranks. The government suppressed us. I demand compensation, policy reform, judicial action, and an apology. There was infiltration by political parties.
166. Statement of Ashok Rai:

On Bhadra 24, I spontaneously joined the protest in Gaushala around 10:00 AM to protest against the shooting and killing of students. The crowd was peaceful, but security personnel gave no warning before using force. I was shot in both knees. I sought treatment independently. I have no photos or videos of the incident. I heard orders to 'shoot, shoot.' The government suppressed us. I seek compensation, policy reform, and judicial action from the Commission. There was infiltration by political parties.
167. Statement of Santosh Chaulagain:

I spontaneously participated in the protest from Maitighar to Baneshwar to demand good governance and action against the corrupt. Security personnel gave no warning. I was struck on the head by a baton and tear gas canister. I do not know their names or ranks, but I can identify them; I have photos and videos. Treatment was independent. I heard orders to 'shoot, shoot,' but do not know the ranks. There was infiltration by political parties.
168. Statement of Suryasan Prajapati:

On Bhadra 24, I spontaneously participated in a protest for good governance at the District Administration Office, Bhaktapur. On the 23rd, I was in Baneshwar. Security personnel included the Nepali Army and Police. They gave no warning. I was hit in the head by a gas canister. I have no photos or videos. Treatment was independent. I seek compensation, policy reform, and judicial action from the Commission.
169. Statement of Ramindra Paswan:

I joined the spontaneous protest against corruption at Gaushala Chowk only on the 24th, during which I was shot in the finger. The protest was unrestrained. There were many security personnel. The shots were fired from a distance of about 12 meters. There was a delay in treatment, but it was not under police surveillance. I did not recognize any security commander; I have no photos or videos. There was infiltration in the protest. I hope for improvements in laws and for the demands of Gen-Z to be met.
170. Statement of Yubaraj Upadhyay:

I spontaneously joined the protest at Baneshwar Chowk for national change and against corruption. The demonstration was peaceful. Security personnel fired shots without any prior warning or appeal for dialogue, hitting me. The distance between the security personnel and us was likely around 30 meters.

Treatment was obtained independently and on time. I can identify the face of the commander who gave the order. I have no photos or videos. I seek compensation, policy reform, and judicial action from the Commission. To prevent such incidents from recurring, corrupt individuals must be prosecuted and jailed. There was infiltration in the movement.
171. Statement of Ram Charmakar:

I spontaneously joined the protest in Kalanki for good governance and against corruption. There was a heavy security presence. The crowd was peaceful. Security personnel fired shots without warning before using force, hitting me in the eye. The distance between the security personnel and us was about 15–20 meters. There is a video of the incident. The state used excessive force. I demand compensation, judicial action, and policy reform. To prevent a recurrence, corrupt individuals must be punished. There was infiltration by political parties. The guilty must be identified and prosecuted.
172. Statement of Suman Bohara:

I spontaneously participated in the protest at Tripureshwar to advocate for the end of corruption and for good governance. The crowd was aggressive, and the presence of security forces posed a danger. Security personnel charged with batons without any warning or dialogue, hitting me in the leg. I received timely medical treatment independently. I do not possess any photos or videos. The state used excessive force. I demand compensation, judicial action, and policy reforms from the state. To prevent such incidents from recurring, corrupt individuals must be punished. There was infiltration in the movement; the culprits must be identified and prosecuted.
173. Statement of Rokaya:

I spontaneously participated in the protest at Baneshwar for good governance and an end to corruption. The crowd was aggressive and the security presence was threatening. Security personnel fired shots without warning, hitting me in the forehead. I received timely treatment independently. I do not have photos or videos. The state used extreme force. I seek compensation, judicial action, and policy reform. Corrupt officials must be punished to prevent future incidents. There was infiltration on the 24th; the guilty must be identified and penalized.
174. Statement of Vijesh Tamang:

I spontaneously participated in the protest from Maitighar to Baneshwar for good governance. The crowd was initially peaceful but became aggressive upon reaching the Parliament building. Police and APF were deployed. I am unaware of any warnings. I was shot in the leg. I received timely treatment independently. I do not have photos or videos. The state used excessive force. I demand compensation, judicial action, and policy reform. Corrupt individuals must be held accountable. There was infiltration; the guilty must be identified and punished.
175. Statement of Kumar Upadhyay:

I work as an accountant at Kumari Oil Stores. I did not participate in the protests against corruption and social media bans on Bhadra 23, nor on the 24th. While I was on the roof of my house, protesters were committing arson at the Gaushala Police Office. As police fired tear gas and bullets, a bullet struck me in the upper arm, penetrating through to my lungs and back. I am under regular medical care. The state has covered all medical expenses. I request the state to provide for my family's livelihood and take action against the guilty.
176. Statement of Badal Rai:

I participated in the protest personally. Initially, the crowd from Maitighar to Baneshwar was peaceful, but it became aggressive once the firing started. Police and APF were on duty from the beginning; the Army arrived later. Before the use of force, there was some information provided regarding the restricted zone. Efforts were made to control the crowd. I sustained a head injury from stone-pelting. The distance between me and the police was only 4–5 meters. Others took me to the hospital. I am receiving good treatment. I do not have photos or videos. The state misused its power. There was infiltration. I have given an interview to Onlinekhabar regarding the incident. Such suppression should not occur during peaceful protests; there should be change according to the spirit of the movement.
177. Statement of Ekta Shah:

I am 17 years old, daughter of Sushil Chand Shah and Bunu Shahi, living in Changunarayan-8. I recently passed Grade 12 and cleared the MBBS entrance exam through the Medical Education Commission; I am currently resting at home. On Bhadra 23, 2082, I spontaneously participated in the protest at the south gate of the Parliament building in Baneshwar. I am unaware of the leadership. During the protest, police first hit me on the right thigh with a baton. Later, I was hit by a rubber bullet in the arm. Since I was in my school uniform, I thought the police wouldn't hit me, so I didn't flee with others and continued chanting slogans. During that time, I was hit by a live round in my left knee and injured. I received free treatment at KMC Sinamangal and B&B Gwarko. I am currently undergoing physiotherapy at home. I face financial difficulties with daily treatment costs. Due to my physical condition, I am unable to travel elsewhere for studies; therefore, I request arrangements for me to study at the nearby KMC Medical College. I also request employment for my guardians.
178. Statement of Shambhu Dangal:

I am the operator and executive editor of Nayapost.com located in Jorpati, Kathmandu. On the morning of Bhadra 23, 2082, around 10:00 AM, we reached Old Baneshwar Chowk while filming a procession of students from Manaslu College and St. Lawrence College in Chabahil. When we reached the Bijulibazar bridge on the Maitighar-Baneshwar road around 11:30 AM, the procession began pushing against the barricades placed by security forces in front of the Everest Hotel. Security forces used water cannons, and the youth knocked down the barricades and rolled them toward the bridge. Around 12:00 PM, the protesters started pelting stones and security forces began firing tear gas. By 12:30 PM, the situation had escalated. The number of riot police on the road was small, while there was a heavy presence of security forces armed with sophisticated weapons inside the Parliament building. The New Baneshwar area reached a state where one could not see or breathe due to tear gas. The number of dead and injured from indiscriminate firing by security forces was rising. While I was filming these scenes around 2:30 PM near the Parliament gate, a metal bullet fired by security forces from inside the Parliament building struck my left leg, piercing through the ankle. I was admitted to the Trauma Center for 16 days. Doctors say it will take another year for my bone to fully heal. The primary responsible parties for the events of Bhadra 23 are the then-government, Prime Minister, Home Minister, and Information Minister. Although the government promised free treatment, only the medicine has been provided; costs for transportation and other essential supplies for the patient have not been covered.
179. Statement of Prakash Bahadur Bohara:

I joined the protest at Maitighar Mandala around 1:45 PM. As I approached Gate No. 1 at Baneshwar Chowk, I was suddenly shot in the thigh, above the left knee. My purpose for joining the protest was to oppose political corruption and advocate for a stable government, a strong economy, and to prevent youth from having to migrate abroad. I joined spontaneously from Maitighar. Initially, the crowd was peaceful, but it became aggressive after I was shot. I did not see security personnel anywhere in the crowd. Police fired without any warning or information about restricted areas. Friends took me to the National Trauma Center by ambulance. I did not see any security commander at the site; video of me being shot is available. I see this as a chaotic abuse of power. I do not run any NGO or INGO. There was no infiltration when the protest started, but as it turned violent, significant infiltration occurred.
180. Statement of Bhim Bahadur Damai:
